title: Modal Verbs
summary: German has six modal verbs that express ability, obligation, permission, desire, and expectation. They are combined with the infinitive of a main verb, which moves to the end of the sentence.

sections.heading: The six modal verbs and their meanings
body: The six German modal verbs are: können (can / to be able to), müssen (must / to have to), wollen (to want to), dürfen (may / to be allowed to), sollen (should / to be supposed to), mögen / möchten (to like / would like to). Möchten is technically the Konjunktiv II form of mögen but is treated as a separate modal at A2 level.

All modal verbs are irregular in the singular present tense: the ich and er/sie/es forms are the same and have no ending, and the stem vowel often changes. The plural forms (wir, ihr, sie) are regular. The table below shows the full present-tense conjugations of können and müssen as models.

examples.en: He wants to buy a car.
We are not allowed to park here.

examples.de: Sie soll um acht Uhr kommen.
examples.en: She is supposed to come at eight o'clock.
I would like to order a coffee.

sections.heading: Sentence structure with modal verbs
body: In a main clause, the conjugated modal verb occupies position 2 (the verb position), and the infinitive of the main verb is placed at the very end of the sentence. This is called the Satzklammer (sentence bracket): the two verb parts form a bracket around the rest of the sentence.

In a subordinate clause introduced by a conjunction such as weil, dass, or ob, the conjugated modal moves to the end and comes after the infinitive: '…weil ich heute arbeiten muss.' In yes/no questions the modal moves to position 1: 'Kannst du mir helfen?'

examples.de: Er kann sehr gut Deutsch sprechen.
examples.en: He can speak German very well.
We have to get up early tomorrow.

examples.de: Ich weiß, dass sie kommen will.
examples.en: I know that she wants to come.

exercises.explanation: The er/sie/es form of können is kann (no ending, stem vowel ö → a).

exercises.question: Which sentence has the correct word order?
exercises.explanation: The conjugated modal (will) takes position 2, and the infinitive (kaufen) goes to the end of the sentence.
The ihr form of dürfen is dürft.

exercises.question: "She is supposed to call her mother." Which modal verb is correct?
exercises.explanation: sollen expresses an obligation or expectation set by someone else: Sie soll ihre Mutter anrufen.
The modal (müssen) takes position 2, the infinitive (machen) goes to the end. The adverb heute follows the modal.
